pol-001.zusammenfassung: The outdoor advertising market is heavily monopolized. A small number of large corporations (JCDecaux, Clear Channel, Ströer, Wall GmbH, Outfront Media) control the majority of advertising spaces in public areas worldwide.
pol-001.kernbefund: This oligopoly position enables these corporations to exert enormous political and economic pressure on municipalities (local governments). Local and small businesses can often not afford advertising space in city centers, meaning that public space primarily carries messages from global brands.
pol-001.relevanz: The dominance of the "big players" leads to a commercialization of public space in which democratic control and local economic structures are weakened.

pol-002.zusammenfassung: Cities increasingly no longer finance public infrastructure (such as bus shelters and public toilets) from tax revenues, but instead hand over their provision to advertising corporations in exchange for long-term advertising rights.
pol-002.kernbefund: These contracts, often concluded for 10 to 15 years, bind municipalities to the advertising industry over the long term. They make it legally difficult or impossible for cities to subsequently regulate or ban outdoor advertising for climate protection or health reasons. JCDecaux and Ströer are pioneers in this model.
pol-002.relevanz: Public space loses its neutrality. Street furniture (Stadtmobiliar) is designed and positioned primarily as an advertising medium, rather than according to the actual needs of citizens.

pol-003.zusammenfassung: The advertising industry invests considerable sums in lobbying at the municipal and national level to prevent or weaken regulations on DOOH (Digital Out of Home) screens.
pol-003.kernbefund: Reports from NGOs such as Corporate Europe Observatory show that outdoor advertisers systematically exert pressure to obtain exemptions from light emission laws (e.g., mandatory shutdown at night) and restrictions on advertising for unhealthy products. The argument frequently made is the alleged financial dependence of municipalities on advertising revenues.
pol-003.relevanz: The political influence of the big players means that health and environmental policy measures to protect citizens are blocked in favor of corporate profits.

pol-004.zusammenfassung: The Cologne (NRW)-based outdoor advertising giant Ströer SE & Co. KGaA uses its financial power and direct political lobbying to secure exclusive, long-term concessions for advertising space on public land.
pol-004.kernbefund: Investigations by investigative NGOs such as Correctiv show that the company's market power is so great that cities effectively have no real alternatives when awarding street furniture contracts (bus shelters). Ströer is also officially registered in the Lobbyregister (the federal lobbying register) and exerts direct influence on urban development policy matters and data protection guidelines.
pol-004.relevanz: The massive local entrenchment of advertising corporations creates a dependency among municipalities. Democratic shaping of public space is undermined by economic monopolies.

pol-005.zusammenfassung: Citizens are increasingly and successfully pushing back against the privatization of public space by outdoor advertising, by strategically using planning and building regulations.
pol-005.kernbefund: Local grassroots organizations (such as the Adblock network in the UK or Résistance à l'Agression Publicitaire (Resistance to Advertising Aggression) in France) systematically monitor municipal planning portals for new applications for DOOH screens and billboards. By mobilizing large numbers of residents and submitting well-founded objections (based on traffic safety, light pollution, and heritage protection), these groups regularly force advertising corporations to withdraw planning applications, or secure rejections from urban planning authorities.
pol-005.relevanz: The advertising industry no longer encounters passive acceptance, but rather organized, informed, and legally effective democratic resistance from civil society.

pol-006.zusammenfassung: The commercialization of public space is encountering massive democratic resistance in Germany, which has manifested in formal legislative procedures.
pol-006.kernbefund: The citizens' initiative "Berlin Werbefrei" (Berlin Ad-Free) collected over 36,000 valid signatures in the first stage of a Volksbegehren (citizens' referendum process) in order to introduce its own far-reaching legislative draft into the Berlin Abgeordnetenhaus (state parliament). The draft envisions radically banning product and consumer advertising in Berlin's public space. Only advertising at the point of sale (e.g., the sign above a bakery), event announcements, and public interest information would remain permitted.
pol-006.relevanz: This initiative proves that a strong, enfranchised civil society rejects the constant visual pressure to consume. Outdoor advertising is increasingly perceived not as harmless decoration, but as a psychological intrusion into public space.

pol-007.zusammenfassung: The aggressive expansion of commercial advertising space is depriving the local arts scene and civic movements of the physical space for non-commercial forms of expression.
pol-007.kernbefund: In many major cities, facades that were previously free or used for community murals (wall paintings) are being taken over by large-format advertising banners or digital screens. This process leads to a cultural homogenization: instead of locally rooted, political or aesthetic art, interchangeable global brand messages appear. Sociological studies show that this weakens residents' identification with their neighborhood, since public space is no longer perceived as a place for co-creation but as a pure consumption zone. The city's "canvas" is sold to the highest bidder instead of serving as a stage for local culture.
pol-007.relevanz: Outdoor advertising is an active displacement factor for non-commercial culture. It replaces diversity with monotony and civic participation with one-directional messaging.

pol-008.zusammenfassung: Berlin sold its advertising rights in public street space in 2018: for the next 15 years (from 2019), the state collects 350 million euros from advertising corporations. Berlin is the most heavily commercialized outdoor advertising city in Germany — 18.5% of total German OOH advertising revenues flow through a single city.
pol-008.kernbefund:
Berlin has **20,643 advertising surfaces** that generate annual poster revenues of **272 million euros**. This represents 18.5% of all German outdoor advertising revenues. The rights were awarded in lots to Wall/JCDecaux (169 large-format illuminated panels + 883 display cases), ILG-Außenwerbung (2,500 Litfaßsäulen (round advertising columns)), Mediateam Stadtservice (4,100 poster sites), and Ströer (430 clock locations). Contract terms: 10–15 years.

The previous model of "infrastructure in exchange for advertising rights" (Wall maintained public toilets and fountains in exchange for advertising space) was replaced by direct payments — with the consequence that the Senate must now fund this infrastructure itself.
pol-008.relevanz: Exclusive contracts of 10–15 years with corporations such as JCDecaux/Wall and Ströer mean: democratically elected city councils cannot, for the duration of these contracts, reduce the number of advertising surfaces, enforce bans on specific product categories, or stop the expansion to digital screens — without risking contractual penalties. Public space is commercially locked up for 15 years.

pol-009.zusammenfassung: Investigative research by CORRECTIV, ZDF Frontal, and Der Spiegel: Between 2016 and 2018, more than 9,400 large-format posters for the AfD (Alternative für Deutschland, a right-wing political party) were booked in approximately 70 German cities via Ströer's booking system — paid for by anonymous donors for over 3 million euros. The AfD was registered in the booking system as a "direct customer." Ströer left the catalogue of questions unanswered.
pol-009.kernbefund: The investigation documents: **over 3 million euros** of anonymous campaign support for the AfD via Ströer surfaces, of which 1.9 million euros were for the 2017 federal election (Bundestagswahl) (approx. 4,900 posters), 521,000 euros for the NRW state election (Landtagswahl), and 221,000 euros for Baden-Württemberg. Party law expert Sophie Schönberger stated: *"We now have proof that the campaign was coordinated at least at the level of the poster producer"* — a possible illegal party donation structure. Ströer regarded itself as a "neutral service provider" and refused to comment.
pol-009.relevanz: Ströer operates advertising surfaces in over 1,200 German cities on the basis of municipal exclusive contracts. As a de facto monopolist for outdoor advertising in many municipalities, the corporation controls which political content is visible in public space — without parliamentary oversight or transparency obligations. The company also owns Statista (a data service) and t-online (a news portal), which extends its influence over public opinion beyond its physical advertising infrastructure.

pol-010.zusammenfassung: Documentation of the creeping commercialization of public space in Hanover (Hannover) by Ströer (subsidiary DSM — Deutsche Städte Medien GmbH): Between 2014 and 2021, Ströer tripled its advertising infrastructure in the city. The city refuses to provide information about contract details, claiming that the companies themselves should communicate their plans.
pol-010.kernbefund:
Ströer's presence in Hanover in 2021 (according to the investigation): **90 City-Star columns, 1,200 large-format posters, 350 Mega-Light installations** (growth from 70 to 210 = ×3 since 2014), **2,200 City-Light post boxes, 11 LED billboards, 200 clock locations, 350 traditional Litfaßsäulen (round advertising columns)**. Origin: In 2009, DSM received approval for 7 City-Star systems in exchange for free election poster spaces at 45 locations. From those 7, the number grew to over 90.

When asked about contract details, the city's press spokesperson replied: *"Companies should communicate their own plans."*
pol-010.relevanz: The Hanover pattern is exemplary for Germany: small initial contract → creeping expansion → monopoly position → opaque contract terms → democratically uncontrollable expansion. Municipalities successively lose control over how their public space is commercially used, because contract details are protected as trade secrets.

pol-011.zusammenfassung: Hamburg sold its public space advertising rights for 508 million euros to JCDecaux and Ströer following a Europe-wide tender in 2007/2009. JCDecaux received approximately 2,150 bus shelters (Fahrgastunterstände) and 350 city information systems (Stadtinformationsanlagen) (2009–2024). The contracts are publicly accessible in Hamburg's Transparenzportal (Transparency Portal) — one of the few German cities that publishes contract details.
pol-011.kernbefund: Hamburg guaranteed advertising corporations **508 million euros** in advertising revenues over the contract term in exchange for street furniture. Although the contracts stipulated that advertising systems were to be integrated into bus shelters, freestanding advertising systems flooded the cityscape — at nearly every major intersection in prominent locations. The Hamburgisches Transparenzportal (Hamburg Transparency Portal) publishes the contract documents for both lots (JCDecaux Lot 1: city information systems; JCDecaux Lot 2: 18/1 large-format advertising installations).
pol-011.relevanz: Hamburg is one of the rare cases in Germany where contract details are publicly accessible. The 508-million-euro guarantee shows: cities effectively grant monopoly rights over their public space in exchange for revenues. During the contract term (15 years), no advertising bans for specific product categories (e.g., fossil fuels, alcohol, junk food) can be imposed without risking contractual penalties. Democratic city politics is circumvented by commercial contract logic.

pol-012.zusammenfassung: In April 2016, the US short-seller Muddy Waters Research (known for uncovering accounting scandals at Chinese companies) published a report accusing Ströer SE & Co. KGaA of serious IFRS violations. The Ströer share price lost over 25% of its value within 45 minutes of publication (falling from €52 to below €35).
pol-012.kernbefund: Muddy Waters claimed that Ströer's reported organic growth, EBITDA, operating cash flow, and free cash flow were **substantially lower than reported** and violated IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards) accounting standards — creating the false impression that the company was financing itself from its own resources. Specifically: digital organic growth rates were allegedly presented in a manipulated manner. Ströer rejected all allegations; the Frankfurt public prosecutor's office subsequently investigated Muddy Waters for possible market manipulation. WirtschaftsWoche (a German business weekly) reported again in 2021 on "suspicious financial conduct" by the corporation.
pol-012.relevanz: Ströer, as Germany's largest outdoor advertiser, operates municipal exclusive contracts in over 1,200 cities. Investors and municipalities entering into long-term partnerships with the corporation face a documented transparency problem: both the financial reporting (Muddy Waters 2016, WirtschaftsWoche 2021) and the political influence (AfD donation scandal 2021) reveal a company that systematically avoids oversight and accountability.

pol-013.zusammenfassung: Jean-Claude Decaux, founder and chairman of JCDecaux, was convicted by the Tribunal de Bordeaux (Bordeaux Criminal Court, France) for "favoritisme" (favoritism/cronyism) and corruption in the awarding of public contracts. The trigger: a contract worth 39.8 million francs (approx. 6 million euros) for school clocks in 204 schools of the Aquitaine Regional Council was awarded without competitive tender to a Decaux subsidiary. Senator Jacques Verlade was convicted of bribery. Decaux received a six-month suspended sentence and a fine of 100,000 francs. He lodged an appeal.
pol-013.kernbefund: The case documents the direct personal involvement of the JCDecaux group chairman in the manipulation of public procurement procedures. Instead of a competitive tender, a Decaux company obtained the contract through personal relationships with an elected politician. The criminal court found the award to be unlawful and issued convictions.
pol-013.relevanz: JCDecaux is the world's largest OOH corporation and is involved in hundreds of municipal exclusive contracts across Europe. The criminal conviction of the founder for manipulation of public procurement procedures is paradigmatic for the business model: cities are dependent on the corporation's contracting practices, and oversight by public institutions has proved inadequate. This case is the earliest documented criminal offense in the company's history.

pol-014.zusammenfassung: FragDenStaat (a German freedom-of-information platform, comparable to "Ask the State") filed a freedom of information request for the disclosure of advertising rights contracts between the BVG (Berliner Verkehrsbetriebe, Berlin's public transport operator) and Wall GmbH (a JCDecaux subsidiary) as well as Ströer. The Berlin Administrative Court ruled in 2019: the financial terms of the contracts do not have to be disclosed — even though the contracts themselves contained a clause stating they held "no trade secrets." The court nonetheless weighed JCDecaux's interest in confidentiality more heavily than the public interest in transparency.
pol-014.kernbefund: The public finances public infrastructure through taxes (BVG buses, trams, metro stations), on which JCDecaux exclusively places advertising. The precise financial terms — that is, how much the city receives for the use of its infrastructure — remain secret. The only publicly known figure: BVG advertising revenues amounted to approximately 10 million euros per year according to the authority's own statements. JCDecaux's original bid from 2006 is reported to have been 103 million euros over the contract term. The contract was renewed in 2021 for a further five years without a public tender.
pol-014.relevanz: The Berlin case shows: even when contracts are expressly not supposed to contain trade secrets, OOH corporations enforce confidentiality through the courts. Democratic control over the monetization of public infrastructure is structurally circumvented. The pattern — secret financial terms, renewals without tender — can be found in Berlin, Hamburg, Frankfurt, and other German cities.

pol-015.zusammenfassung: A W&V (Werben & Verkaufen, a German advertising industry trade publication) investigation (2017) found: Ströer DSM held outdoor advertising rights in Frankfurt at a fixed lease rate of only €409,000 annually plus revenue sharing — a total of approximately €2.6 million per year for the city. By comparison: Hamburg had re-tendered its OOH rights in 2007 and has since received approximately €34 million per year. Frankfurt was thus receiving, by calculation, **1/13 of the Hamburg market price** for comparable-sized public space.
pol-015.kernbefund: The disparity — €2.6 million vs. €34 million for comparable municipal advertising rights — demonstrates how strongly municipal revenues depend on negotiating power and transparency during the award process. Frankfurt was contractually obligated to re-tender by January 1, 2018. The study implies that without this comparative pressure, many German cities are permanently ceding public space to OOH corporations at far below market value — without democratic oversight of the foregone value.
pol-015.relevanz: This example illustrates the systemic pattern: OOH corporations secure fixed prices far below market value through long-term initial contracts. When contracts expire after 10–20 years, political and institutional dependencies have built up that make a market-rate re-award difficult. The foregone public value — in Frankfurt's case potentially several hundred million euros over a 15-year contract term — is not transparently reported.

priv-001.zusammenfassung: Modern digital advertising screens (DOOH) are increasingly equipped with cameras and sensors that capture biometric data and movement profiles of passers-by in order to deliver targeted advertising ("programmatic") to specific audience segments.
priv-001.kernbefund: Investigations by NGOs show that companies use technologies for Automated Facial Detection (AFD) as well as Wi-Fi sniffing to analyze the age, gender, mood, and dwell time of individuals in front of screens. This occurs in public space, usually without the active consent (opt-in) of those affected, raising serious data protection and ethical concerns.
priv-001.relevanz: Outdoor advertising is evolving from a passive broadcast medium into a proactive surveillance infrastructure in public space. Citizens can barely escape this tracking (opt-out illusion), since they must move through public space.

priv-002.zusammenfassung: The linking of physical advertising locations with digital identity markers enables seamless tracking of citizens in public space.
priv-002.kernbefund: Modern DOOH kiosks (Digital-Out-of-Home) frequently use Wi-Fi and Bluetooth sensors to passively capture the unique MAC addresses or mobile advertising IDs of passers-by. This data is cross-referenced with location data from apps and third-party providers to measure the so-called "path-to-purchase": tracking whether a person who passed a poster later enters the advertised store or searches for the product online. NGOs such as Big Brother Watch criticize that this cross-referencing occurs without the informed consent of citizens and transforms public space into a private surveillance network.
priv-002.relevanz: DOOH is no longer merely an advertisement, but an active sensor in surveillance capitalism. Anonymity in urban space is being systematically abolished through the technological upgrading of advertising infrastructure.

priv-003.zusammenfassung: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) has clarified that capturing personal characteristics (age, gender, emotions) through camera systems in public advertising installations without valid consent is unlawful. The programmatic DOOH business model, which is based on audience measurement, is therefore structurally incompatible with the GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, known in German as DSGVO — Datenschutz-Grundverordnung).
priv-003.kernbefund: DOOH screens use camera systems for the anonymized counting and classification of passers-by (Anonymized Video Analytics / AVA). The EDPB establishes: even when no biometric templates are stored, the processing of facial and bodily characteristics for audience analysis constitutes processing of personal data pursuant to Art. 4 GDPR. In public space, obtaining valid, informed consent from all those affected is practically impossible.
priv-003.relevanz: Programmatic DOOH booking platforms (e.g., Broadsign, Vistar Media, The Trade Desk) sell "Audience Targeting" for outdoor advertising. This de facto requires that passers-by be analyzed for their demographic characteristics. Companies that book programmatic DOOH expose themselves to considerable GDPR liability risk — particularly following recent million-euro fines from EU data protection authorities.

priv-004.zusammenfassung: Clear Channel Outdoor Americas launched the program "RADAR" in 2016: anonymized location data from mobile phones (via partners such as AT&T, Placed, PlaceIQ) are linked with the reach of Clear Channel billboards in order to analyze the movement patterns of passers-by. US Senator Charles Schumer publicly described the system as "spy billboards." Senator Al Franken formally requested written information on consent mechanisms, data sharing, and security measures. RADAR was subsequently also rolled out in Europe (UK, Spain, Sweden).
priv-004.kernbefund: RADAR uses mobile phone location data to create behavioral patterns and movement profiles of people in public space — without their knowledge and without direct consent. Privacy advocates criticized: the claim of "anonymization" is unverifiable. In Europe, RADAR was adapted for GDPR compliance; the fundamental problem — commercial surveillance of public spaces without active consent — remains structurally unchanged. DOOH displays effectively function as data points in a private surveillance network.
priv-004.relevanz: Clear Channel is one of the three largest OOH corporations worldwide with an extensive European presence. RADAR demonstrates: DOOH is not merely an advertising medium, but an instrument for mass surveillance of public space. Its use as surveillance infrastructure occurs without democratic decision-making — through long-term municipal exclusive contracts, this infrastructure is removed from democratic access.

psych-001.zusammenfassung: Media content — particularly advertising — promotes unrealistic body ideals in children and adolescents. The study documents the causal link between media exposure and the development of eating disorders such as anorexia nervosa and bulimia nervosa.
psych-001.kernbefund: Body dissatisfaction in children and adolescents is directly associated with media consumption and leads to reduced quality of life, deteriorated mood, and unhealthy eating habits. Advertising that portrays unrealistic ideals of thinness is a primary trigger.
psych-001.relevanz: Advertising in public space is unavoidable: children and adolescents are exposed to it daily on their way to school, on public transport, and in shopping zones, with no possibility of opting out. Unlike television or online advertising, outdoor advertising cannot be clicked away or switched off.

psych-002.zusammenfassung: Self-objectification through advertising exposure is linked to body shame, depression, anxiety, and eating disorders. The study analyzes the psychological mechanisms through which advertising content damages the self-image of young people.
psych-002.kernbefund: Exposure to beauty and diet content from advertising accelerates the development of eating disorders. A report by the Center for Countering Digital Hate (2023) found that algorithmically delivered content worsened body image anxiety in 70% of adolescents. Outdoor advertising permanently transports the same images into public space.
psych-002.relevanz: Beauty advertising on billboards and digital screens in public space displays persistent, idealized images to which young people are exposed daily on their routes — with no control over frequency or content.

psych-003.zusammenfassung: Advertising tactics designed to compel attention cause consumers to perceive manipulative intent — thereby reducing advertising effectiveness, brand attitude, and purchase intent. Yet studies show that even recognized manipulation still influences purchasing behavior.
psych-003.kernbefund: When advertising is perceived as manipulative, attitudes toward the advertisement, attitudes toward the brand, and purchase intent all decline significantly. This demonstrates that outdoor advertising is deliberately designed to compel attention — and in doing so violates the cognitive autonomy of passers-by.
psych-003.relevanz: Outdoor advertising deliberately employs stimuli that involuntarily capture attention: garish colors, motion (on digital screens), provocative imagery. In public space, there is no way to circumvent this manipulation.

psych-004.zusammenfassung: Neuroscientific research confirms that subliminal messages can influence decisions under certain conditions — particularly when they align with existing needs or desires. These effects are demonstrable over the long term.
psych-004.kernbefund: Subliminal messages can steer decisions in a particular direction without the affected person noticing. Advertising deliberately exploits these mechanisms: brief image sequences, subliminal messages, and emotional conditioning in outdoor advertising influence behavior below the threshold of conscious perception.
psych-004.relevanz: Digital advertising billboards with changing imagery, brief animations, and emotional visual language use the same mechanisms. Unlike online advertising, these influences in public space cannot be blocked or avoided.

psych-005.zusammenfassung: Empirical study examining the influence of subliminal advertising on purchasing behavior. Non-marketing experts and young consumers are particularly susceptible to unconscious influence through advertising messages.
psych-005.kernbefund: Subliminal advertising measurably influences purchasing behavior, especially among groups without media literacy training. Children and adolescents, as well as individuals without marketing knowledge, are most severely affected.
psych-005.relevanz: Outdoor advertising in public space is directed at the entire population — including the most vulnerable groups (children, people without media literacy). There are no warning systems or protective mechanisms whatsoever.

psych-006.zusammenfassung: Field studies on outdoor advertising show that creativity in advertising visuals is deliberately deployed to hijack involuntary attention and create brand memory through context-dependent memory mechanisms.
psych-006.kernbefund: Outdoor advertising deliberately exploits the orienting response mechanism (unconscious attentional orientation toward novel stimuli), context-dependent memory formation, and cognitive fluency. Creative advertising thereby overcomes a cognitive "attention threshold" — meaning it is designed so as to compel involuntary attention.
psych-006.relevanz: Advertising professionals explicitly design outdoor advertising so that it involuntarily draws the gaze of passers-by — that is its stated purpose. This deliberate manipulation of involuntary cognition in public space occurs without the consent of those affected.

psych-007.zusammenfassung: The psychology behind digital screens (DOOH) is based on the exploitation of evolutionary reflexes. Unlike static posters, changing or moving images compel the human brain to look.
psych-007.kernbefund: The so-called orienting response (Orientierungsreflex) is an involuntary neurological mechanism that compels people to pay attention to sudden changes in their peripheral visual field (such as changes in light or motion on a screen), since this is evolutionarily associated with danger. DOOH installations deliberately exploit this reflex to "steal" attention. One cannot decide not to look.
psych-007.relevanz: Digital advertising billboards are psychologically far more aggressive than traditional OOH (out-of-home) posters. They represent a permanent cognitive stressor in public space, as they keep the brain in a constant state of alertness.

psych-008.zusammenfassung: Modern advertising screens are equipped with sensors that scan the audience in real time in order to psychologically optimize advertising messages.
psych-008.kernbefund: An increasing number of DOOH installations integrate camera systems that capture the faces of passers-by using "Anonymized Video Analytics" (AVA). The technology analyzes in fractions of a second the age, gender, dwell time, and in some cases even the emotional reaction (mood) to the displayed advertising. Although the industry claims not to store images, the analysis occurs without the knowledge or consent of passers-by. This "eye tracking" in public space is used to perfect the effectiveness of manipulation techniques and to systematically undermine the cognitive defenses of citizens.
psych-008.relevanz: Public space is becoming a laboratory for the advertising industry. Passers-by are involuntarily turned into test subjects in a mass-psychological experiment in which every reaction is biometrically recorded and commercially exploited.

psych-009.zusammenfassung: Peer-reviewed analysis of the long-term consequences of advertising exposure on children: advertising instills materialistic values that are in direct conflict with prosocial behavior, intrinsic motivation, and psychological resilience.
psych-009.kernbefund: Children who are intensively exposed to advertising demonstrably develop stronger materialistic attitudes — the equation of possessions with happiness, status, and identity. This correlates with lower self-esteem, increased dissatisfaction, and reduced life satisfaction in adulthood. Outdoor advertising in public space is particularly effective at producing this conditioning, as it represents a permanent, non-consent-based exposure.
psych-009.relevanz: Unlike TV advertising, OOH/DOOH in public space cannot be switched off — children on their way to school, while playing, or in the city cannot escape this exposure. The psychological conditioning through materialistic messages thus takes place without parental oversight or control.
